text: The laparoscopic view shows a four-centimeter umbilical hernia at the umbilicus, with visible omentum adhered to the hernia sac.

The surgeon observes the bowel component within the hernia sac, noting the absence of any dilated bowel loops. The omentum is gently pushed to reduce its presence in the sac, despite its incarceration.

The surgeon uses a small sponge in the left hand to apply pressure and perform gentle blunt dissection on the omentum. If necessary, the harmonic scalpel is used to dissect the omentum, facilitating the release of the hernia neck and allowing the remaining fat to protrude.
The surgeon performs slow dissection to separate the entire hernia sac, ensuring that all components of the omentum are removed from the sac while leaving the natural fat of the peritoneum intact.

The surgeon performs dissection to separate the adhered omentum during the laparoscopic repair of a ventral hernia. The omentum is carefully detached, revealing the hernia content completely. A polyurethane dual mesh is then placed, with the white side facing the abdominal wall and the blue side facing the bowel. Gore-Tex Tads are visible at the four corners of the mesh.

The surgeon displays a composite mesh with white polypropylene and blue polyurethane components. The white side is positioned towards the abdominal wall, while the blue side faces the bowel to reduce adhesion risk.
The surgeon stretches the synthetic coated mesh across the surgical field, ensuring all four corners are evenly positioned without any corrugation.

The surgeon uses a suture to secure the mesh, with the knot positioned subcutaneously. A ProTac device, made of titanium, is employed to fix the mesh in place, creating both an outer and inner crowning. The mesh is stabilized as fibrosis occurs, and the peritoneum covers it. The surgeon ensures the abdominal wall is perpendicular to the ProTac tip for deeper penetration without reaching the skin.

The surgeon carefully avoids firing the ProTac device directly over the hernia defect, ensuring it is applied around the defect while avoiding the inferior epigastric vessels. The two medial umbilical ligaments are visible, and the ProTac is applied on either side, staying medial or lateral to the inferior epigastric vessels. The inner crowning is performed with caution to prevent the ProTac from piercing the thin skin over the defect, reducing the risk of infection.

The surgeon completes firing the ProTac implants around the mesh to secure it in place during the laparoscopic repair of an umbilical hernia. The mesh is checked for stability, and additional ProTac implants are fired if necessary.
